questions.question: In the Claude Code architecture, when the model emits a tool_use block for an MCP-sourced tool, what happens at the host layer?
options: The model connects directly to the MCP server
The host routes the call to the correct MCP server via JSON-RPC tools/call — the model has no awareness of whether the tool is native or MCP
The host converts the tool_use into a REST API call
The MCP server intercepts the model output directly
questions.explanation: The model sees all tools — native and MCP — as identical tool definitions. It has no awareness of the source. The host maintains the routing table: it maps tool names to either native execution or MCP server JSON-RPC calls. This abstraction is what makes MCP transparent to the model.

questions.question: Skills expand what the model KNOWS, tools expand what it can DO, and MCP expands what it can CONNECT to. What's the one thing none of them change?
options: The model itself — it's still a stateless text transformer, text in, text or tool_use out
questions.explanation: This is the core mental model: the model is a stateless function. Tools, skills, and MCP expand what it can perceive and affect, but the model itself doesn't change. Same weights, same architecture, same stateless text-in/text-out behavior. Everything is built around it, not inside it.

questions.question: A skill file and an MCP resource both provide information to the model. What's the key architectural difference?
options: Skills are static files loaded deterministically by the host; MCP resources are dynamic data served by an external process via the MCP protocol
MCP resources can only contain JSON
Skills work offline while MCP resources require internet
questions.explanation: Skills: static files, deterministic path lookup, zero infrastructure. MCP resources: dynamic data served by a running process, can be subscribed to for live updates, served via JSON-RPC. Use skills for curated instructions that rarely change. Use resources for live data from external systems.
